coredata.dc:title: Curcumin-derived water-soluble carbon dots without detectable resistance: Dual potentials for antimicrobial activity and infected wound healing

coredata.dc:description: Due to the abuse of traditional antibiotics, the increasing prevalence of antibiotic resistance poses a significant challenge to the finding of novel antimicrobial materials. Curcumin, a natural polyphenolic compound, exhibits broad-spectrum antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties. However, its therapeutic utility is limited by its bioavailability. To improve the biological activity of curcumin, the highly water-soluble carbon dots (E-CDs) were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using curcumin and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (Na2EDTA) as precursors. It was shown that the surfaces of E-CDs still remain the structural and functional groups of their precursors. The as-obtained E-CDs exhibited a higher antimicrobial efficacy against Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (E. coli) with the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of only 40 and 150 ppm, respectively. Strikingly, the E-CDs did not induce significant bacterial resistance while exhibiting excellent biocompatibility. In a mouse model of bacterial-induced wound infection, the wound closure had approached 60 % within just four days, highlighting their significant potential for accelerating wound healing. Our experimental results convincingly demonstrated that the E-CDs are a promising candidate for clinical applications in treating infected wounds and combating drug-resistant bacterial infections.

head.ce:title: Curcumin-derived water-soluble carbon dots without detectable resistance: Dual potentials for antimicrobial activity and infected wound healing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anhui Key Laboratory of Chemo-Biosensing, Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu 241000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the abuse of traditional antibiotics, the increasing prevalence of antibiotic resistance poses a significant challenge to the finding of novel antimicrobial materials. Curcumin, a natural polyphenolic compound, exhibits broad-spectrum antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties. However, its therapeutic utility is limited by its bioavailability. To improve the biological activity of curcumin, the highly water-soluble carbon dots (E-CDs) were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using curcumin and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (Na2EDTA) as precursors. It was shown that the surfaces of E-CDs still remain the structural and functional groups of their precursors. The as-obtained E-CDs exhibited a higher antimicrobial efficacy against Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (E. coli) with the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of only 40 and 150 ppm, respectively. Strikingly, the E-CDs did not induce significant bacterial resistance while exhibiting excellent biocompatibility. In a mouse model of bacterial-induced wound infection, the wound closure had approached 60 % within just four days, highlighting their significant potential for accelerating wound healing. Our experimental results convincingly demonstrated that the E-CDs are a promising candidate for clinical applications in treating infected wounds and combating drug-resistant bacterial infections.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, bacterial infections are increasingly prevalent, which pose a serious threat to public health, food safety and security, and environmental hygiene conditions [1]. These ubiquitous microorganisms are constantly threatening human life and health and indirectly cause tremendous economic losses to society [2–4]. However, the abuse of antibiotics has become more and more prominent in recent years [5,6]. It is of concern that bacteria have progressively evolved so that they can effectively impair the cumulative effect of antibiotics by decreasing the ability to penetrate the cell or accelerating the cytosolic mechanisms [7,8], which eventually causes the concentration of antibiotics in the bacterial body to drop sharply to nearly ineffective levels [9]. This process not only alters the stability of the original chemical structure of antibiotics [10,11] but also prevents the normal formation of biofilms, which poses a significant challenge to traditional antimicrobial therapy [12]. Therefore, it is imperative for scientists to develop new antimicrobial materials with high efficiency, low toxicity, and intrinsic antibacterial resistance [13,14].

Curcumin, a unique natural polyphenolic compound, can be extracted from the spice turmeric [15]. Due to its superior antioxidant and anti-inflammatory performances as well as its broad-spectrum antibacterial and antiviral properties [16,17], curcumin can inhibit the formation of bacterial biofilm while strengthening the body's defense mechanism against bacterial infections [18,19]. However, the bioavailability of curcumin is severely limited because of its poor stability and low aqueous solubility [19–21].

In recent years, numerous techniques have been proposed to improve the stability and solubility of curcumin. The most popular one is chemical modification or wrapping with additional molecules [22,23]. Behbahani et al. described a successful encapsulation technique that uses microemulsion sonication to enhance the stability of curcumin in the stomach [24]. To improve the solubility and stability, Zhao et al. employed curcumin to esterify carboxylated chitosan [25]. However, these strategies exhibit a compromised performance on increasing their antimicrobial properties.

Carbon dots (CDs), as an emerging nanomaterial, have attracted much attention due to their good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and easy surface functionalization. They not only show promising applications in bioimaging but also show great potential in the development of novel antimicrobial agents. Different synthesis methods give rise to different size, shape, and surface chemistry of CDs, which significantly influence their antimicrobial effects [26]. The antimicrobial mechanisms of CDs include direct disruption of bacterial cell membranes, generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), and inhibition of bacterial metabolism [27]. In addition, CDs can serve as carriers for antibiotics and other antimicrobial compounds, improving efficacy and reducing the risk of drug resistance. So far, several types of antimicrobial CDs have been designed, such as graphene quantum dots [28], nitrogen-doped CDs [29], and metal ion-modified CDs. However, the antimicrobial CDs still face the challenges of long-term stability and in vivo safety [27]. To address these issues and further enhance the antimicrobial efficiency, curcumin-based carbon dots (E-CDs), which combine the advantages of the natural polyphenol curcumin and the chelating agent Na2EDTA, are proposed in this study, aiming to provide a safe and stable antimicrobial material to overcome the current limitations and enhance the antimicrobial efficiency.

The curcumin-based CDs not only significantly improve the solubility and antimicrobial activity of curcumin in aqueous solution, but also enhance the stability and bioavailability through chemical modification and nanoencapsulation techniques [30]. Notably, because of their extraordinary features, such as a higher specific surface area, more stable chemistry, and a greater electron transfer capacity, heat-treated carbon dots show improved potential for enhancing antimicrobial efficacy [31–35].

In this work, we successfully synthesized novel carbon dots (E-CDs) by a one-step hydrothermal method using curcumin and Na2EDTA as raw materials. Na2EDTA, as a highly efficient chelating agent, not only significantly improves the aqueous solubility of the resulting materials but also disrupts the cell wall by recognizing and acting on the bacterial surface. The generated E-CDs inherited the structural features of Na2EDTA and curcumin, which exhibited superior solubility and prominent antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (E. coli), even in the absence of light stimulation conditions. In addition, in a mouse model of bacterial-induced skin injury infection, the topical treatment with E-CDs could operate directly on the wound surface and significantly accelerate the wound healing process, suggesting that the E-CDs have a broad prospect in clinical applications.

2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein (DCFH-DA, 97 %), cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8), fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC), and propidium iodide (PI) were purchased from Shanghai Biyuntian Bio-Tech Ltd. Co. S. aureus (ATCC25923, Gram-positive) and E. coli (ATCC25922, Gram-negative) strains were purchased from Shanghai Beyno Bio-technology Co. SPF-grade male C57 mice (6–8 weeks of age) were purchased from Nanjing Qinglongshan Animal Breeding Farm Co. Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (Na2EDTA), curcumin, tetracycline, agar, yeast extract, peptone, and other routine chemicals were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co. All of the chemicals were used as received without further purification. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (resistance >18.25 MΩ cm−1).

The nanoscale microstructure of the materials was observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-F200). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out using a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using a Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha. The structure of the materials was analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, BRUKER INVENIO). The fluorescence spectra were recorded by a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, F-7000). Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, TCS SP8) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi 8100) were employed to observe the bacteria. The zeta potentials of E-CDs were characterized using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS ZEN3700.

The E-CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method. Typically, 0.2 g of curcumin and 0.2 g of Na2EDTA were dispersed in 20 mL of water to form a suspension. Afterwards, the above mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon reactor for a continuous pyrolysis reaction at 180 °C for 6 h. After the resultant solution was centrifuged, the supernatant was filtered using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and further dialyzed by a dialysis membrane (MWCO 500) for 48 h. After the purified E-CDs were freeze-dried, the as-collected powder was weighed and the yield was calculated (yield = 15.3 %), and subsequently stored at 4 °C away from light for further application.

To investigate the optimal antimicrobial properties of E-CDs, several E-CDs were fabricated through the same procedure, while the only exception is that the mass of Na2EDTA was modulated to be 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, and 0.6 g, respectively. The resulting four samples were denoted as E-CDs 2:1, E-CDs 1:1, E-CDs 1:2, and E-CDs 1:3, respectively.
Curcumin carbon dots (Cur-CDs) were synthesized by the same steps using curcumin as the sole precursor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacteria culture and assays of antibacterial activity in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: All the experimental equipment and bacterial culture media were sterilized, and antimicrobial tests were carried out using optical density measurement (OD600), the agar plate method, as well as the disc diffusion method. S. aureus and E. coli were inoculated into LB medium containing different concentrations of E-CDs and incubated in 96-well plates [36]. OD600 was determined after 24 h of incubation. Subsequently, the bacterial suspensions were appropriately diluted and coated on agar plates for 16 h of constant incubation at 37 °C [37].

The bacterial suspension (1 × 108 CFU/mL) was spread evenly on the agar medium. The paper sheets containing different materials were then evenly placed on the agar culture plates. The diameter of the inhibition bands around each disk was determined after incubation at 37 °C for 18 h [38].

Morphological characterization of untreated and E-CDs-treated S. aureus and E. coli was performed using SEM. First, the bacterial cultures were mixed and incubated with the E-CDs solution at 37 °C for 6 h. After centrifugation, the bacteria were immobilized in a 2.5 % glutaraldehyde solution overnight. Then, the bacterial suspension was dried by dehydration treatment with gradient ethanol (30, 50, 70, 90, and 100 %) before dropping it on a silicon wafer [39]. Finally, the morphology of S. aureus and E. coli was observed using SEM.

Animal experiments followed the approval of the Animal Care Committee of Anhui Normal University (AHNU-ET2022028) and conformed to the National Research Council's Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals. The S. aureus infection model was carried out according to previous reports with slight modifications [40]. Circular wounds were incised on the backs of mice with medical scissors, and the mice were randomly divided into two groups. The wounds were coated with S. aureus (20 μL) and covered with PBS-moistened sterile cotton for 24 h to ensure the growth of bacteria. After 24 h, the mice were treated with the E-CDs and pH 7.4 PBS once every two days, and the recovery of wounds in each group was investigated, compared, and photographed on the days 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12. ImageJ software was used to calculate the trauma area. The weight changes of the mice were recorded to detect the health status of the mice during the wound-healing process.

To explore the cytotoxicity of the E-CDs, the cell viability was detected using Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8). Alpha Mouse Liver 12 (AML12) cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium in 5 % CO2 gas at 37 °C.

The E-CDs samples (final concentrations of 0, 5, 10, 20, 40, 80, 160, and 320 ppm) were co-incubated with the cultured cells for 12 h. The treated cells were inoculated in 96-well plates and incubated with 10 μL of CCK-8 solution (2 mg/mL) for 4 h. Finally, the absorbance at 450 nm was determined by an enzyme meter, and the cell viability was calculated. Each experiment was repeated 3 times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of intracellular ROS in bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: The levels of ROS were detected on a fluorescence spectrometer using DCFH-DA fluorescent staining as an indicator. The specific process was as follows: S. aureus (or E. coli) was incubated with the E-CDs and Na2EDTA solution (40 and 150 ppm, respectively) at 37 °C for 3 h. Then, the suspensions were centrifuged, and the bacteria were collected, washed twice, resuspended in pH 7.4 PBS buffer, and incubated with DCFH-DA (2 mL, 10 mM) for 30 min at 37 °C. Finally, the fluorescence intensity (λex/λem = 488 nm/525 nm) was measured to assess ROS generation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of bacterial resistance to E-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The initial minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) values of tetracycline and the E-CDs against S. aureus and E. coli were determined as described in Section 2.4. Subsequently, a bacterial dilution (~106 CFU/mL) was prepared with a 1/2 MIC concentration of 96-well plate bacteria for the next MIC assay. The above bacterial suspensions were incubated with the E-CDs and control tetracycline, respectively. After incubation at 37 °C for 16 h, the new MIC values were obtained. This process was repeated until generation 12, and the MIC values for each generation were recorded and analyzed in comparison with generation 1 to assess the drug resistance.

For live/dead staining of E. coli and S. aureus, the bacterial culture was the same as the operation in Section 2.4. After 12 h, the bacteria were collected by centrifugation and rinsed with PBS, subsequently stained with FITC (10 μL) and PI (20 μL) for 45 min in the dark. The bacteria were then centrifuged at 6000 rpm and washed three times with PBS to remove excess dye. Finally, the bacteria were resuspended in fresh PBS buffer and imaged on slides by CLSM [41,42].

The antioxidant capacity of E-CDs was assessed by DPPH assay [43]. Specifically, after adding 500 μL of DPPH ethanol solution (100 μM) to 500 μL of E-CDs solution at different concentrations, the mixture was incubated in the dark for 1 h at 25 °C. Subsequently, the absorption spectra were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer in the range of 450–750 nm. The DPPH scavenging capacity of E-CDs was determined by the following equation [44]:
DPPH scavenging capacity =
A a
−
A s
/ Aa × 100 %
where Aa and As represent the absorbance values of the samples in the absence and presence of E-CDs, respectively (λ = 517 nm).

Biofilm biomass was assessed by crystal violet staining (CVS) [45]. First, bacterial suspensions were inoculated in 96-well plates and incubated at 37 °C for 48 h to promote biofilm formation. The well plates were washed several times with PBS, and then different concentrations of E-CDs solutions (80 ppm and 300 ppm) were added; subsequently, incubation was continued at 37 °C for 24 h. The well plates were again gently washed with PBS, followed by staining with 0.1 % crystal violet for 30 min. At the end of staining, excess dye was removed by rinsing twice with PBS and dried naturally at room temperature. Finally, ethanol was added to each well to dissolve the stained biofilm, and then the OD value of the solution at 570 nm was measured using an enzyme marker.

To evaluate the hemocompatibility of the E-CDs, 2 mL of mouse blood was taken, and erythrocytes were collected by multiple centrifugations. After that, 100 μL of erythrocyte suspension was added to 900 μL of different concentrations of E-CDs solution and incubated at 37 °C for 3 h [46]. Subsequently, the erythrocytes treated with PBS buffer and deionized water were used as negative and positive controls, respectively. Finally, the mixture of erythrocyte and sample was centrifuged, and the supernatant was taken to determine its absorbance at 540 nm, and the hemolysis rate was calculated [47].

The specific schematic diagram of the synthesis process is displayed in Scheme 1 . The E-CDs with various mass ratios (curcumin: Na2EDTA) were synthesized by the hydrothermal method. The antimicrobial evaluation showed that the E-CDs with a mass ratio of 1:1 exhibit the best antimicrobial effect, thus they were characterized and applied in detail. For the sake of clarity, unless otherwise stated, in the following text, “E-CDs” specifically refers to the E-CDs with the ratio of curcumin: Na2EDTA 1:1.

The as-prepared E-CDs have excellent dispersibility (the 10 mg/mL of E-CDs solution is clear and transparent), whereas the 2 mg/mL natural curcumin aqueous suspension is not homogeneous. The Cur-CDs were completely soluble at a concentration of 5 mg/mL, indicating that the hydrothermal treatment can improve the water solubility of precursor curcumin. The size variation of carbon nanomaterials not only reflects the physical properties but also significantly affects their antimicrobial properties [48–50]. The formation of E-CDs was attributed to the carbonization of curcumin and Na2EDTA [51]. The E-CDs are monodispersed spheres, and statistical analyses extracted from the images show that the average particle sizes are 5.5–7.5 nm (Fig. 1a). The HRTEM image (Fig. 1b) displays that the crystallographic spacing of E-CDs is 0.24 nm, corresponding to the graphitic carbon (100) facet [52].

The carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) elements in the E-CDs were identified by XPS (Fig. 2a-d). As shown in Fig. 2a, the contents of C, N, and O in the E-CDs are 65.8 %, 7.7 %, and 26.5 %, respectively, indicating that the nitrogen element was successfully doped in the E-CDs. The valence bonds and contained groups of the three elements, C, N, and O, in the E-CDs were further examined. The absorption peaks of C1s at 284.8, 286.4, and 288.3 eV corresponded to C-C/C-N single bonds, O-C=O bonds, and C-O-C single bonds, respectively (Fig. 2b) [53]. The N1s have three absorption peaks at 399.6, 401.6, and 402.4 eV, corresponding to NH, NC, and -N+(CH3)3, respectively (Fig. 2c) [54]. The three absorption peaks of O1s located at 531.3, 532.8, and 533.7 eV were attributed to the OC single bond, O=C-OH double bond, and C-OH single bond (Fig. 2d) [55], respectively.

The functional groups of E-CDs were characterized by FTIR. As illustrated in Fig. S1, both E-CDs and curcumin exhibited broad phenolic hydroxyl absorption peaks in the range of 3500–3200 cm−1, indicating that the -OH groups were not implicated in the hydrothermal process. However, compared with pure curcumin and Na2EDTA, the CC, -OH, CO, NC, and NH stretching and/or bending peaks in the range of 800–3500 cm−1 changed inconspicuously, indicating that the structural and functional moiety was remained on the E-CDs surfaces even after the hydrothermal treatment [30]. As reported, the antimicrobial effect of curcumin was associated with the phenolic hydroxyl functional group [56]. As a result, we believe that the -OH group in the E-CDs might be responsible for the antimicrobial ability. The excellent hydrophilicity and a good dispersibility of the E-CDs were demonstrated by several characteristic absorption peaks, including the broad OH stretching vibration peak in the range of 3500–3200 cm−1, the NH stretching vibration peak at 3030 cm−1, and the CO stretching vibration at 1635 cm−1 [57].

The crystal structure of E-CDs was further investigated by XRD. As shown in Fig. S2, the high diffraction peaks of Na2EDTA suggests that the microstructure of precursor is organized. Pure curcumin exhibits a lot of sharp peaks in the range of 10–30°, being attributed to hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking interactions among curcumin molecules, which result in a greater crystal polymorphism in the solid [30]. In contrast, only a broad diffraction peak centered at 26° was observed in the XRD spectra of E-CDs, which is consistent with the graphitic carbon (002) diffraction surface of the disordered carbon atoms [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antimicrobial activity of E-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial growth was performed to evaluate the antimicrobial activity of E-CDs, curcumin, Cur-CDs, and Na2EDTA [59]. As shown in Fig. 3 and S3, the MIC of curcumin, Cur-CDs, and Na2EDTA was 500 ppm, 400 ppm, and 100 ppm against S. aureus, and 1000 ppm, 800 ppm, and 250 ppm against E. coli, respectively, which suggests that the antimicrobial activity of Cur-CDs is still relatively limited. As a comparison, the MIC of the E-CDs 2:1, 1:1, 1:2, and 1:3 was all considerably reduced (Fig. S3). Among them, the E-CDs 1:1 exhibited the strong antimicrobial activity, with MIC as low as 40 ppm against S. aureus and reduced to 150 ppm against E. coli (Fig. 3c). The difference in inhibitory effect of the E-CDs is probably due to the cellular structures of E. coli and S. aureus, i.e., E. coli has a thicker cell wall with an extra outer lipid membrane than S. aureus, which makes it harder for the antibacterial agent to penetrate [60–62]. To evaluate the long-term antibacterial ability of the E-CDs, the different concentrations of E-CDs were mixed and incubated with S. aureus and E. coli for 72 h, respectively (Fig. S3e and j). The results showed that the E-CDs could consistently inhibit the growth of these two bacteria at concentrations of 40 ppm and 150 ppm for 72 h, confirming the long-term inhibitory effect of E-CDs on the bacteria.

The sterilizing efficacy of E-CDs was qualitatively assessed using the plate counting method [63,64]. As shown in Fig. 4 , both E. coli and S. aureus both thrived on the curcumin-containing plates. The quantity of bacterial colonies was somewhat decreased with the addition of Na2EDTA. However, E. coli and S. aureus were notably unable to survive on face masks with the E-CDs. Overall, the E-CDs show discernible antimicrobial activity against E. coli and S. aureus, in agreement with the MICs.

In addition, the sterilizing effect of E-CDs was investigated by disc diffusion tests. As shown in Fig. S4, for S. aureus and E. coli, an unobvious inhibitory zone was observed at 300 μg of curcumin, indicative of the faintest activity of curcumin at this dose. In comparison, the diameter of the bacteriostatic zone on the Mueller-Hinton agar plate with 300 μg of E-CDs was greater than that of Na2EDTA (300 μg), indicating that the E-CDs have an outstanding antibacterial ability. Additionally, in the case of the E-CDs, a notable inhibition zone of 22 mm against S. aureus was observed when compared with E. coli, confirming a superior inhibitory effect against Gram-positive bacteria.

The disruptive effect of E-CDs on bacterial cells was observed using SEM imaging. As shown in Fig. 5 a and c, in the control groups, S. aureus and E. coli preserved their intact bacterial structure with smooth cell membrane integrity, and uniform density of intracellular substances [65]. Compared to the control groups, a more severe disruption of cell membrane integrity was observed after the E-CDs treatment (Fig. 5b, d), which resulted in a greater degree of intracellular substances leakage and aggregation [66].

To further investigate the possibility of bacterial membrane damage by the E-CDs, PI and FITC staining experiments were conducted to observe both dead and live bacteria (Fig. 6 ). The FITC bacterial staining plots displayed numerous green fluorescent dots (Fig. 6a-d), demonstrative of an abundance of bacteria. It should be noted that FITC staining cannot be employed to distinguish between dead and live bacteria [67]. Thus, PI staining assay was implemented to label the dead bacteria. As shown in Fig. 6e and g, there was no obvious red fluorescence when S. aureus and E. coli treated with PI in saline, indicating that the majority of the bacteria were alive. After treatment with the E-CDs for 4 h, however, the PI-stained graphs displayed intense red fluorescence (Fig. 6f and h), signifying that many bacteria were dead and the E-CDs were effective in killing bacteria. It should be stated that as a fluorescent dye that is impervious to cell membrane, PI can only enter dead or membrane-broken cells and produces red fluorescence once it has embedded itself in nucleic acid [66,68]. Therefore, these findings suggest that the E-CDs are capable of efficiently rupturing the bacterial membrane structure [69].

To further explore the antimicrobial mechanism of the E-CDs, the levels of ROS in the bacteria were examined via the DCFH-DA fluorescent staining method [70]. After exposure to the E-CDs, S. aureus and E. coli strains displayed the greatest rise in intracellular ROS levels, as demonstrated by DCFH-DA staining (Fig. S5a and b). According to our findings, the E-CDs were significantly more capable of causing damage to proteins, lipids, nucleic acids, and metabolites than Na2EDTA [71]. It is commonly recognized that Na2EDTA can specifically identify and impact bacterial lysosomes and lipopolysaccharides, thereby effectively rupturing the integrity of bacterial cell walls and membranes and generating bacteriostatic effects [72]. Curcumin, as a potent antioxidant, has a remarkable capacity to scavenge free radicals. Compared to the strong antioxidant performance of curcumin, the E-CDs after hydrothermal treatment showed a substantial decrease in antioxidant capacity, and free radical scavenging efficiency was significantly reduced (Fig. S6). This result suggests that the hydrothermal treatment process may alter the chemical structure or functional groups of curcumin, thereby affecting its antioxidant properties. Furthermore, the zeta potential of E-CDs was measured to be +9.5 mV (Fig. S7), and their surface positive charges resulted in strong and ongoing interaction with negatively charged bacteria, increasing oxidative stress and electrostatic interactions between the E-CDs and bacterial membranes.

Taken together, the positively charged E-CDs exhibited a high adhesion tendency to the cell membrane of negatively charged bacteria, physically destroying the integrity of the bacterial membrane and leading to rupture and perforation. Then, the entry of E-CDs caused the generation of ROS, and subsequently the bacteria were internally disorganized and eventually died. Therefore, the E-CDs had obvious antibacterial advantages over conventional antibiotics. The enhanced antimicrobial potency of E-CDs could originate from the synergistic bacteriostatic effects of the structural and functional moieties of curcumin and Na2EDTA maintained in the E-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibiofilm properties, drug resistance, and safety investigation of E-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial biofilm is a major factor in drug resistance, formed by bacteria and their secreted extracellular polymers adhering to the surface, which acts as a barrier to protect the bacteria from immune attack and antibiotic penetration, leading to therapeutic failure [45]. To investigate the effect of E-CDs on bacterial biofilms, crystal violet staining was used to assess the degree of biofilm disruption under different treatments, with the depth reflecting the amount of biofilm biomass. The results showed that the E-CDs significantly disrupted the biofilms of S. aureus and E. coli, and the disruption of S. aureus biofilm was especially obvious (Fig. S8a). The content of biofilm was quantified by measuring the absorption value at 570 nm (Fig. S8b). It was found that the survival of S. aureus biofilm was reduced to 16.6 % and that of E. coli biofilm was <40 % after treatment with the E-CDs. This suggests that E-CDs have a significant destructive effect on biofilms and are particularly more effective on S. aureus biofilms. These results not only show the long-lasting antimicrobial performance of the E-CDs but also demonstrate their potential to combat biofilm formation, providing strong support for the E-CDs as the novel antimicrobial materials.

To investigate whether the E-CDs could progressively induce bacterial resistance, we examined the MIC values of 12 generations of S. aureus and E. coli cultivate with sub-inhibitory concentrations of the E-CDs and using tetracycline drugs as a positive control [73] (Fig. 7 ). The MICs of tetracycline against S. aureus and E. coli increased steadily in 12 generations of bacterial inhibition, with a brief plateau during the period [74]. In contrast, the MICs of E-CDs remained unchanged throughout the 12 generations, suggesting that the S. aureus and E. coli were unsusceptible to causing the evolution of resistance to E-CDs. This may be related to the antibacterial mechanism of the E-CDs, i.e., the positively charged E-CDs contacted with bacteria through electrostatic binding and then penetrated the bacteria to stimulate ROS generation within the bacteria. In general, the antimicrobial mechanism is due to specific interactions between antibiotics and bacterial units, which were more likely to raise antimicrobial resistance evolution [75]. Because of their distinct antimicrobial function, the E-CDs, a novel class of antimicrobial material, show considerable promise and practical prospects in avoiding and regulating bacterial drug resistance.

The CCK-8 assay was performed in vitro safety studies to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the E-CDs on AML 12 [76]. With >85 % cell viability, the E-CDs in the range of 5–320 ppm demonstrated less toxicity to AML 12 cells in comparison to the controls (Fig. S9a). Substantially, the cytotoxicity of E-CDs was significantly less than the safety threshold in the aforesaid antimicrobial assays, as the concentration of E-CDs was much lower than 320 ppm. An in vitro hemolysis assay was performed to verify whether the E-CDs induced erythrocyte lysis and evaluate their hemocompatibility. As shown in Fig. S9b, when erythrocytes were added to sterilized double-distilled water, the solution after centrifugation was red in color, indicating that the change of intra- and extracellular osmotic pressures in the sterilized double-distilled water would lead to the rupture of erythrocytes. In comparison, when the erythrocytes were dispersed in different concentrations of E-CDs, the solutions were almost colorless and transparent after centrifugation, and the erythrocytes could be deposited at the bottom of the centrifuge tube. While the concentration of E-CDs reached 320 ppm, the hemolysis rate was only 3.1 % (< 5 %) compared with the negative control. The result showed that the E-CDs can't cause erythrocyte rupture and hemolysis under physiological conditions, indicative of a good blood compatibility. Therefore, these findings also provide an important safety rationale as potential antimicrobial materials for biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo healing of infected wounds by the E-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the antimicrobial capacity in vivo and the ability of E-CDs to promote wound healing, S. aureus was employed to infect fresh wounds in mice. Fig. 8a illustrates the construction of the wound infection model and the process of therapy. The experimental group was treated with the E-CDs, whereas the infected wounds were filled with pH 7.4 PBS as a control group. As shown in Fig. 8b, in the early stage (Day 2), the wound site in the control group was infected by bacteria with inflammation, while the mice wounds in the E-CDs group tended to heal, and the wound area started to shrink due to the superior antimicrobial capacity of the E-CDs. On Day 4, the E-CDs infected wound closure had approached 60 % and began to crust and form a protective covering. On Day 12, the wound of the E-CDs group was nearly fully healed. From Day 2 to Day 12, the E-CDs showed the remarkable improvement in wound healing as compared to the control group (Fig. 8c, d). To investigate whether E-CDs cause toxicity during the treatment of traumatic wounds, the changes in body weight of mice were recorded throughout the experiment. Fig. 8e showed that there was no discernible variation in body weight changes among treatments, indicating that the E-CDs had no harmful side effects [77].

In summary, the E-CDs synthesized from curcumin and Na2EDTA by a one-step hydrothermal process maintained the essential functional groups and structures of their precursors, giving them a number of advantageous characteristics such as small size, high water solubility, enhanced bioavailability, and potent antimicrobial effects. Efficient bactericidal activity was produced by the positively charged E-CDs effectively attaching to bacteria, rupturing their cell membranes, and stimulating the generation of ROS. Notably, the E-CDs show good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity to normal cells (AML 12), and no discernible bacterial resistance. The topical application of E-CDs conspicuously expedited wound healing without any negative side effects, according to an in vivo study of a mouse model of S. aureus-induced wound infection. These findings highlight a significant clinical potential of the E-CDs as an effective tool for wound healing and antimicrobial therapy.
